title: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
description: A post-training alignment regime that uses human preference data to steer a pretrained model toward responses people rate as more helpful, safe, or on-policy.
openingSummary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback is a post-training workflow that starts with a pretrained model, gathers human judgments about better and worse answers, turns those judgments into a learned preference signal, and then updates the model so its behavior moves toward what people prefer.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, usually shortened to RLHF, is a post-training regime for shaping model behavior after pretraining. Teams begin with a base model that already knows language, then use human comparisons to teach the system which answers feel more helpful, safer, or more on-policy than the alternatives.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: Pretraining teaches a model to continue text, but it does not automatically make the model concise, harmless, or aligned with product policy. RLHF exists because teams often need a second stage that pushes a capable base model toward instruction following, safer refusals, and answers that better match what users or product teams actually want.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The workflow usually moves in four steps. First, a pretrained or supervised-fine-tuned starting policy generates candidate answers for prompts. Second, human raters rank which answers are better or worse. Third, those rankings train a reward model or other preference estimator that stands in for the human judgments. Fourth, a reinforcement-learning optimizer updates the policy while keeping it from drifting too far from the starting model.

comparedToNearbyRegimes.title: Compared To Nearby Regimes
comparedToNearbyRegimes.body: RLHF is one alignment regime inside the broader alignment toolbox. It differs from plain supervised fine-tuning because it optimizes against preferences instead of only copying demonstrations. In practice, Proximal Policy Optimization, or PPO, is the classic optimizer inside the RLHF loop, while Direct Preference Optimization, or DPO, and Group Relative Policy Optimization, or GRPO, are nearby preference-training approaches that try to remove or simplify parts of the full reward-model-plus-reinforcement-learning pipeline.

limitationsAndFailureModes.title: Limitations And Failure Modes
limitationsAndFailureModes.body: RLHF can be expensive because it needs human preference data, repeated model sampling, and a reward model that is good enough to stand in for people. The loop can also be slower to iterate than simpler post-training methods because each update depends on fresh rollouts and extra evaluation passes. If the learned reward misses what users really want, optimization can become unstable, the policy can game the reward, or the model can narrow its behavior into answers that look safe in training but feel rigid or evasive in real use.

trainingFlow.alt: An RLHF loop from a pretrained policy to sampled answers, human rankings, a learned reward model, and a policy update.
trainingFlow.caption: RLHF turns human comparisons into a learned preference signal, then updates the policy against that signal.

update.label: Policy update
